title: Setagaya Family Murder: Japan's Coldest Case 🔍
segments: On December 30, 2000, the Miyazawa family went to bed inside their home in the Soshigaya district of Setagaya, Tokyo. Mikio, 44, his wife Yasuko, 41, daughter Niina, 8, and son Rei, 6. By the following morning, they were dead. The case has remained open for more than two decades, despite one of the largest investigations in modern Japanese history.

The killer entered through a second-floor bathroom window. He climbed a tree on the side of the home, slipped inside, and went straight for Rei first. The boy was strangled in his bed. Mikio came up the stairs and was attacked on the staircase. Yasuko and Niina were attacked next.
What investigators found inside the house puzzled them from the start. After the murders, the killer didn't leave. He stayed for hours.
He used the family computer. Logs showed someone accessed the household PC at 1:18 a.m. on December 31. The suspect browsed websites tied to a theater company that had been using the lot next door.

He ate ice cream from the freezer. Four containers were emptied. He drank from bottles in the fridge. Investigators recovered partial DNA from saliva left on the cans and from sweat soaked into clothing.
The killer used the bathroom and didn't flush. Forensic teams collected fecal matter, which gave investigators a clear genetic profile. They also found traces of sand on the floor.

That sand is one of the strangest pieces of evidence in the case. Lab analysis showed it contained minerals consistent with the Mediterranean coast, Nevada deserts, and parts of Edwards Air Force Base in California. It did not match any common Japanese soil sample.
Detectives suspected the killer either traveled internationally before the murder or had recently been at a US military facility. That theory has driven decades of follow-up work, with no resolution.

Footwear evidence was equally unusual. Bloody prints in the home matched Korean-made Slazenger sneakers in size 28 cm — large for a Japanese man. The shoes had only been distributed in limited numbers across South Korea.

The clothing the killer left behind was a logoed Uniqlo fleece, a hat, gloves, and a fanny pack. He apparently changed clothes inside the house. Investigators believe he was injured during the attack on Mikio, who fought back hard.
Blood from a different person was on the kitchen sink and on a towel. That blood produced the second key DNA profile in the case. It belongs to the killer himself.

The Tokyo Metropolitan Police have run the DNA against millions of samples. Genetic analysis suggests the suspect's paternal line traces to southern Europe or the Mediterranean, while the maternal line traces to East Asia.

Here's where the case gets stranger. The fanny pack contained sand that did not match the sand on the floor. It contained a black hair from someone unrelated to the family. And the tickets and items inside hinted at travel through multiple regions.

More than 280,000 investigators have worked the case across 24 years. The Tokyo police have collected over 12,000 pieces of physical evidence — the largest evidence file in Japanese cold case history.
There were no signs of forced robbery. Money was scattered around the home but most of it was untouched. The killer took roughly 150,000 yen and left tens of thousands more in plain sight.

Investigators believe the murders were not financially motivated. The savagery of the attack, the time spent in the home afterward, and the targeted nature of the violence point to something personal — but no link between the family and any suspect has surfaced.

A theater group had been performing on a vacant lot adjacent to the Miyazawa home. The lot was scheduled for demolition in early 2001. Some theorists believe the killer was tied to the dispute over that lot.
Mikio's brother also lived next door, in a building owned by their father. A few suspected the killer mistook the homes. That theory has been weighed and largely set aside.
The suspect made no effort to hide his identity. He left his clothes, his shoes, his blood, his DNA, his fingerprints, and even feces. Whoever he was, he wanted to disappear — and he did.
In 2017, Tokyo police released new sketches and renewed appeals worldwide, including in South Korea. They offered a reward of 20 million yen.

Genealogical DNA testing — the same approach used to identify the Golden State Killer — has been applied to the Setagaya samples. Japanese privacy law has limited certain follow-up steps, but international cooperation has expanded.
On the 24th anniversary of the case in December 2024, Tokyo Metropolitan Police chief Akihiro Onuma issued a public appeal for tips. The reward stands. The DNA profile still has no match.

A simple fact haunts every investigator who has touched the file. The killer ate ice cream while the family lay dead upstairs. He sat at a computer and surfed the web for hours. Then he walked away into the morning.
If you have any information about the Miyazawa family, the Tokyo Metropolitan Police continue to accept tips through their cold case division. Even a small detail can break this case.
